PUBH 7462 Week 13 activity: Parallel bootstrapping

Author

Eric Lock

Objective

The goal of this lab is to move beyond serial execution. You will implement a computationally intensive bootstrap simulation and compare the performance of standard R loops against a parallelized backend.

The Task: Bootstrapping a Non-linear Model

We will estimate the sampling distribution of parameters for a non-linear decay model: \[y = a \cdot e^{-bx} + \epsilon\]

Because Non-linear Least Squares (NLS) requires an iterative numerical solution for every resample, it is significantly more demanding than linear regression, making it an ideal candidate for parallelization.

1. The Setup

First, generate a large synthetic dataset (\(n=10000\)) and fit an initial model:

library(stats)
set.seed(123)
n <- 10000
x <- runif(n, 0, 10)
y <- 5 * exp(-0.5 * x) + rnorm(n, sd = 0.2)
df <- data.frame(x, y)

# Fit to simulated data, using starting values:
start_vals <- list(a = 1, b = 1)
fit <- nls(y ~ a * exp(-b * x), data = df, start = start_vals)
a.est=summary(fit)$coefficients[1,1] #estimate for a
b.est=summary(fit)$coefficients[2,1] #estimate for b

2. Serial Execution

Perform \(B = 1,000\) bootstrap resamples using a standard for loop or lapply. Use system.time() to record how long it takes.

Tip: Use tryCatch() inside your loop, as NLS may occasionally fail to converge on certain resamples.

3. Parallel Execution

Refactor your code to run in parallel using the doParallel and foreach packages.

  1. Detect and Register: Use detectCores() to see what you have, then register the backend. I suggest using one less than your total number of cores, just to not overload your computer.

    library(doParallel)
    Loading required package: foreach
    Loading required package: iterators
    Loading required package: parallel
    n_cores <- parallel::detectCores()
n_cores
    [1] 10
    registerDoParallel(cores = n_cores-1)

  2. Foreach Implementation: Use foreach with the %dopar% operator.

    • Use .combine = 'rbind' to return a data frame of the estimated a and b for each bootstrap sample.
    • Use .packages = 'stats' to ensure nls is available to the workers.

  3. Cleanup: When finished, shut down the workers:

    stopImplicitCluster()

4. Plot the bootstrap results

Make a simple histogram of the bootstrapped estimates for a and b, to check the results. They should look something like this:

5. Compare computing time and experiment

Was the parallel approach substantially faster than the serial approach? Try rerunning both approaches with different sample sizes ‘n’. You might find that when n is smaller (e.g., n=100) the serial approach is actually faster, because each step is quick and the estimation time is dominated by the overhead of parallelization rather than the computation itself. When n is very large (e.g., n=30000) the estimation can take substantial time, and you are likely to see more benefits of parallelization.

Upload your R script on Canvas to receive credit for this activity.
